Name: RGSR
LongName: Reporting Guidelines for Simulation-Based Research in Social Sciences
Description: A reporting framework for simulation-based research in the social sciences, introduced with a focus on system dynamics research. The guideline separates minimum and preferred requirements for reporting models, simulation experiments, optimization experiments, and key visualization practices needed to support reproducibility and efficient communication.
Aim: Improve reproducibility, transparency, and efficient communication in simulation-based social science manuscripts by requiring enough information to recreate models, repeat simulation experiments, and reproduce optimization analyses.

Scope.Coverage: Simulation-based research manuscripts in the social sciences, especially those using system dynamics and related computational modeling approaches to generate numerical results.
Scope.Focus: Model structure and equations, base-case reproduction, units, data sources, source code access, simulation platform and algorithm details, scenario specifications, uncertainty and stochastic reporting, optimization objective and search procedures, and visualization choices that reduce the cost of independent reproduction.
Scope.ContrastWith: RGSR is broader than a single modeling-platform checklist and is focused on manuscript reporting for reproducibility. It does not prescribe how a system should be modeled or analyzed, and it is not limited to one simulation paradigm.

primary.description: Rahmandad H, Sterman JD. Reporting guidelines for simulation-based research in social sciences. System Dynamics Review. 2012;28(4):396-411.

items.item_name: Avoid non-informative full-model diagrams
items.item_question: Does the manuscript avoid including large, cluttered diagrams whose main effect is to signal complexity rather than explain the model or its insights?
items.item_question_background: The paper argues that poor visuals raise the cost of reproduction and can actively confuse readers. Its first visualization warning is aimed at so-called gazinta diagrams that display a tangle of structure without helping readers understand the model.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether the manuscript uses large all-in-one causal or structure diagrams, and note whether those figures clarify the model or function mainly as cluttered complexity displays.

source_page_refs.quote: avoid “gazinta diagrams” whose only function is to show readers
Such diagrams contribute nothing to the presentation of your model

items.item_name: Present causal relationships without visual clutter
items.item_question: Are causal relationships shown clearly, with unnecessary crossings, overlaps, and ambiguous polarity signs avoided?
items.item_question_background: The authors treat clarity of causal presentation as part of reproducibility because visual confusion slows or distorts reconstruction of the model. Their examples focus on avoiding crossing links, overlapping names, and invisible polarity markings.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether causal diagrams use multiple views when needed, avoid overlapping labels and crossing links, and keep polarity signs legible.
items.item_expected_location: Model figures / Causal loop diagrams / Stock-flow diagrams

source_page_refs.quote: Avoid clutter in presenting causal relationships of a model
Make sure polarity signs are visible

items.item_name: Use summary visuals when full causal diagrams are infeasible
items.item_question: When the full model is too large for the main text, does the manuscript use summary visuals such as subsystem diagrams, model-boundary charts, causal loop diagrams, or simplified stock-flow maps?
items.item_question_background: Rather than either omitting structure or dumping the entire model into the paper, the guideline recommends summary visuals that preserve interpretability under space constraints. This is about making the model communicable even when full documentation lives elsewhere.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any use of subsystem diagrams, model boundary charts, simplified stock-flow maps, or other summary visuals when the full model is documented outside the main text.

source_page_refs.quote: Use subsystem diagrams, model boundary charts, causal loop diagrams
when, due to space limitations, presenting the causal diagrams for the full model is infeasible

items.item_name: Keep graph content focused and readable
items.item_question: Do graphs focus on the key quantities being discussed and avoid unnecessary chart junk such as default gridlines or indistinguishable line encodings?
items.item_question_background: The visualization guidance is not just about diagrams of structure; it also addresses output graphs. The paper emphasizes that overloaded graphs and weak line differentiation make reported results harder to assess and reproduce.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether graphs limit displayed information to key outputs, remove unnecessary gridlines or decorative elements, and use line styles or colors that remain distinguishable in print and grayscale.

source_page_refs.quote: Limit the amount of information reported in graphs to the key items
Make sure different lines portraying variables in graphs of simulation output are distinguishable

items.item_name: Explain model operations in plain text
items.item_question: Does the manuscript explain in plain language what computational operations the model is designed to perform?
items.item_question_background: The minimum model reporting requirement starts with intelligibility. Before equations can be implemented, readers need a plain-text account of what the model does so they can understand the logic of the computational structure they are trying to recreate.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the prose explanation of the model's computational operations, process logic, and intended behavior.

source_page_refs.quote: computational operations the model is designed to perform shall be explained in plain text

items.item_name: Report equations and algorithmic rules
items.item_question: Does the manuscript provide the equations and algorithmic rules needed to implement the model independently?
items.item_question_background: Plain-language explanation alone is not enough for reproduction. The guideline treats formal model specification as indispensable because independent researchers must be able to reconstruct the computational machinery, not just the narrative description.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all reported equations, update rules, decision rules, and algorithmic procedures needed to implement the model.

source_page_refs.quote: documentation includes equations and algorithmic rules
sufficient to allow an independent party to implement and simulate the model

items.item_name: Report model parameters and initial values
items.item_question: Does the manuscript provide all model parameters and initial values needed to recreate the base-case model?
items.item_question_background: Even when equations are available, reproduction fails if the numerical values used to initialize and parameterize the model are absent. The paper highlights this as one of the most common reasons published models cannot be rebuilt.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all parameter values, initial conditions, baseline exogenous inputs, and any table or appendix that defines the base-case model numerically.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Parameter tables / Appendix / Online supplement

source_page_refs.quote: all model parameters and initial values

items.item_name: Report units for variables and parameters
items.item_question: Are units of measurement reported for all variables and parameters?
items.item_question_background: The authors explicitly elevate units into the minimum requirement because they see units as indispensable for building and understanding system dynamics models. Units help catch specification errors and make numerical relationships interpretable across implementations.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the units reported for each model variable and parameter, or note where units are missing.
items.item_expected_location: Equation tables / Appendix / Model documentation tables

source_page_refs.quote: Units of measurement shall be reported for all variables and parameters

items.item_name: Report only changes when extending a previously compliant model
items.item_question: If the model extends a previously published publicly accessible compliant model, does the manuscript clearly describe the changes from that earlier model?
items.item_question_background: The guideline allows efficient reporting when work builds directly on an already documented model. In that case, reproducibility hinges on clearly isolating the modifications rather than redundantly restating the entire prior model.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the citation to the prior compliant model and the specific structural, parametric, or algorithmic changes introduced in the current study.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Relation to prior model / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: only the changes from the previously reported model need to be described

items.item_name: Provide sources of data for equations and algorithmic rules
items.item_question: Does the manuscript provide the qualitative or quantitative data sources underlying equations and algorithmic rules?
items.item_question_background: The preferred model requirement goes beyond structural reproducibility and aims to support assessment of whether the model is grounded appropriately. Knowing where equations and rules came from helps reviewers evaluate plausibility, calibration, and transferability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract citations, datasets, elicitation sources, or qualitative evidence used to justify equations, parameterizations, and algorithmic rules.

source_page_refs.quote: Sources of data (qualitative or quantitative) for different equations and algorithmic rules

items.item_name: Define variables and explain formulation logic
items.item_question: Does the manuscript define all model variables and explain the logic behind their formulation?
items.item_question_background: The paper distinguishes raw equation listing from intelligible documentation. Variable definitions and formulation logic matter because they let readers understand why the formal specification takes the shape it does rather than treating equations as opaque symbols.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract variable definitions, conceptual meanings, and explanations of why each major formulation or functional form was chosen.

source_page_refs.quote: Definition of all the variables used in the model and the logic behind their formulation

items.item_name: Provide source code in the original implementation platform
items.item_question: Does the manuscript make the model source code available in the original implementation platform, preferably in a freely accessible and executable format?
items.item_question_background: The preferred model requirement treats original-platform code as a practical accelerator for assessment and reproduction. The authors specifically emphasize accessible formats because reproduction is hindered when code exists but cannot be opened or run by others.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether source code is shared, the implementation platform, file formats provided, and whether the files can be accessed and executed freely.
items.item_expected_location: Code availability / Supplementary materials / e-companion / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: Source code in the original implementation platform
preferably in a format that can be freely accessed and simulated

items.item_name: Report the simulation software and hardware platform
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the software and hardware platform used for the simulation experiments?
items.item_question_background: The minimum simulation requirements begin with platform disclosure because reproducibility in computational research can depend on software environment and hardware details, especially when implementations differ across systems.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the simulation software, version, operating system, hardware description, and processor information reported for the experiments.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Footnotes to results tables / Appendix / Supplementary materials

source_page_refs.quote: The software and hardware platform(s) used for the simulation

items.item_name: Report the simulation algorithm
items.item_question: Does the manuscript specify the simulation algorithm used, such as the integration method, time step, meshing method, or event-prioritization scheme relevant to the model type?
items.item_question_background: The paper highlights algorithmic settings as a major source of hidden variation across implementations. Reporting them is necessary because two researchers can start from the same model equations yet generate different results if numerical solution methods differ.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the simulation algorithm, numerical integration method, time step, meshing method, event-priority scheme, or equivalent implementation choices relevant to the model.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Simulation setup / Table footnotes / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: The simulation algorithm used, such as integration method and time step

items.item_name: Report preprocessing needed before simulation
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report any preprocessing needed on the base-case model before the reported simulation experiments can be reproduced?
items.item_question_background: The guideline explicitly mentions preprocessing because published results may depend on steps performed before the main simulation run, such as generating exogenous inputs. If these are omitted, nominally complete model documentation can still be unreproducible.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any preprocessing steps, scripts, transformations, or generated exogenous inputs required before running the reported experiments.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Data preparation / Appendix / Supplementary methods

source_page_refs.quote: Any pre-processing ... needed on the base-case model

items.item_name: Report scenario parameter settings
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the parameter settings required to reproduce each reported scenario?
items.item_question_background: Simulation studies often hinge on scenario variation rather than a single base case. The paper therefore requires scenario-specific parameter settings so readers can recreate the exact experimental design used to generate reported comparisons.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract parameter values for each reported scenario, including any scenario tables or appendices that define the experimental conditions.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Scenario tables / Results table footnotes / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: Parameter settings required to reproduce any reported scenario

items.item_name: Report Monte Carlo parameter distributions and sampling procedures
items.item_question: When Monte Carlo simulation is used, does the manuscript report the joint parameter distributions, generating equations or correlation structure, and the sampling procedure?
items.item_question_background: The article singles out Monte Carlo work because stochastic scenario generation introduces extra layers of hidden design. Reproducibility depends not only on which parameters vary, but on how they are jointly distributed and sampled.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract distributional forms, generating equations, correlation matrices, and the sampling procedure used for Monte Carlo or other stochastic parameter draws.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Uncertainty analysis / Monte Carlo setup / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: for Monte Carlo simulations, the joint distributions for the selection of parameters
along with the sampling procedure used

items.item_name: Report the number of iterations per scenario
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the number of iterations run for each scenario?
items.item_question_background: Iteration count matters because stochastic stability, uncertainty measures, and computational cost all depend on how many runs were performed. The paper therefore treats iteration count as a separate minimum reporting element rather than something readers should infer.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the number of iterations for each scenario or experimental condition, including any ensemble size or repetition count.

source_page_refs.quote: The number of iterations per scenario

items.item_name: Report post-processing steps applied to simulation output
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report all post-processing steps used to turn raw simulation output into the reported results?
items.item_question_background: The paper recognizes that reported tables and figures often depend on transformations after simulation ends. Without those reductions, aggregations, or regressions, an independent researcher may reproduce the raw runs but still fail to reproduce the published findings.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all data reduction, aggregation, summary-statistic, regression, or other post-processing steps applied to simulation outputs before reporting results.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Data analysis / Appendix / Supplementary methods

source_page_refs.quote: All post-processing steps ... used to transform simulation outputs to reported results

items.item_name: Report sensitivity analysis on algorithmic parameters
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report whether sensitivity analysis was conducted on algorithmic parameters such as time step or simulation method?
items.item_question_background: The preferred simulation requirements are partly about helping readers assess robustness rather than just rerun code. Sensitivity analysis on numerical settings is important because substantive results can be artifacts of the algorithm rather than of the modeled system.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any sensitivity analysis on time step, simulation method, meshing choices, or other algorithmic parameters, including what settings were tested.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Robustness checks / Table footnotes / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: If any sensitivity analysis was conducted on robustness of the algorithmic parameters

items.item_name: Report computational costs for simulation experiments
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report computational costs such as simulation time and processor information when those costs are meaningful?
items.item_question_background: The authors include computational cost as a preferred element because it helps readers judge feasibility and scale. This matters especially when experiments are large enough that replication effort or resource requirements could otherwise be underestimated.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract simulation runtime, processor information, and any other reported computational-cost details for the experiments.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Table footnotes / Appendix / Supplementary materials

source_page_refs.quote: Information on computational costs, including simulation time and processor information

items.item_name: Report random number generation details for stochastic models
items.item_question: For stochastic models, does the manuscript report the random number generation algorithm and the noise seed or equivalent stream specification?
items.item_question_background: Stochastic simulation cannot be understood fully without knowing how pseudo-random variation was generated. The guideline therefore asks for both the generator and the seed or stream specification so readers can evaluate or approximate the realized randomness.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the random number generation algorithm, seed, stream identifiers, or other parameters that determine pseudo-random draws in stochastic models.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Stochastic model setup / Table footnotes / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: The random number generation algorithm used and the noise seed

items.item_name: Report uncertainty measures and how they were calculated
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report uncertainty measures for stochastic or Monte Carlo results and specify how those measures were calculated?
items.item_question_background: The guideline does not treat uncertainty summaries as self-explanatory. It explicitly asks authors to state both the uncertainty metric and the method used to compute it, because identical-looking intervals can come from different assumptions and procedures.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract reported standard deviations, confidence intervals, or other uncertainty measures, along with the method used to compute them.
items.item_expected_location: Results tables / Methods / Table footnotes / Appendix

source_page_refs.quote: A measure of uncertainty ... in reported statistics in stochastic models and Monte Carlo analysis
The method used to calculate confidence intervals and other measures of uncertainty should be fully specified

items.item_name: Report the optimization software environment
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the software environment in which the optimization was implemented?
items.item_question_background: The optimization section extends the simulation requirements because optimization procedures add another layer of platform dependence. Knowing the software environment is necessary to reconstruct how the search was actually executed.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the optimization software, module, package, or platform used to implement the optimization procedure.

source_page_refs.quote: The software environment in which the optimization has been implemented

items.item_name: Report the optimization objective or payoff function
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the payoff or objective function being maximized or minimized as a function of model variables?
items.item_question_background: Optimization results are uninterpretable without the target being optimized. The paper treats the objective function as a core reproducibility element because it defines what counts as a better solution and how search results should be evaluated.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the optimization objective function, payoff function, loss function, or calibration target, including how it is defined from model variables.

source_page_refs.quote: The payoff function to be maximized (minimized)

items.item_name: Report the optimization search space
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the parameter space over which the optimization search is conducted and, when needed, how search parameters map into model variables?
items.item_question_background: The guideline separates the search target from the region being searched. Reproducibility requires the bounds and mapping of the search space because an algorithm can only be evaluated properly if readers know what alternatives it was allowed to consider.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract search bounds, admissible parameter ranges, constraint definitions, and any mapping from search parameters to model variables.

source_page_refs.quote: The parameter space over which search for the best payoff value is conducted

items.item_name: Report the search algorithm and any modifications
items.item_question: Does the manuscript identify the optimization search algorithm by reference and explain any modifications or new search methods used?
items.item_question_background: The authors recognize that optimization procedures are often adapted in practice. They therefore require both a citation to the underlying algorithm and a clear explanation of any deviations that would matter for reproduction.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the search algorithm used, the citation to its original source, and any modifications, constraints, or novel search procedures applied in the study.

source_page_refs.quote: The search algorithm used shall be specified by reference to the original article
fully explaining any modifications or new search methods

items.item_name: Report convergence iterations for iterative optimization methods
items.item_question: If iterative optimization methods are used, does the manuscript report the number of iterations needed for convergence?
items.item_question_background: For iterative search methods, the path to the reported solution matters as well as the endpoint. The guideline asks for convergence counts because they help readers assess computational effort and whether the reported solution was obtained under stable stopping behavior.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the number of iterations to convergence for iterative optimization or equilibrium-finding procedures.

source_page_refs.quote: the number of iterations needed for convergence shall be reported

items.item_name: Report the realized search effort behind the optimization result
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report the actual search effort that produced the optimization result, such as restarts, simulated scenarios, and iterations per scenario?
items.item_question_background: The paper does not stop at specifying the algorithm in principle; it also asks for the actual search performed. This matters because optimization results can depend heavily on how extensively the parameter space was explored in practice.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the number of search restarts, total scenarios simulated, iterations per scenario, and any other details describing the realized search effort.

source_page_refs.quote: The actual search that has led to the reported optimization results

items.item_name: Provide optimization implementation code
items.item_question: Does the manuscript provide the implementation code or control files needed to reproduce the optimization procedure quickly?
items.item_question_background: The preferred optimization requirements focus on reducing friction in reproduction. Implementation code and control files matter because optimization workflows can be much harder to recreate from prose alone than ordinary simulation runs.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether optimization implementation files, control files, or platform-specific setup code are shared and where they can be accessed.
items.item_expected_location: Code availability / Appendix / e-companion / Supplementary materials

source_page_refs.quote: Optimization implementation codes

items.item_name: Report computational costs for optimization experiments
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report optimization time and processor information for each optimization experiment?
items.item_question_background: Optimization can be substantially more resource-intensive than ordinary simulation. The guideline therefore recommends reporting optimization runtime and processor details so others can judge replication effort and performance realistically.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract optimization runtime, processor information, and any computational-cost details associated with each optimization experiment.

source_page_refs.quote: Information on computational costs, including optimization time and processor information

items.item_name: Report uncertainty in estimated parameters for calibration studies
items.item_question: For calibration or estimation results, does the manuscript report uncertainty in the estimated parameters?
items.item_question_background: The preferred optimization section recognizes that point estimates from calibration can be misleading without uncertainty. Reporting confidence or uncertainty intervals helps readers assess how tightly the data constrain the fitted parameters.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any confidence intervals, standard errors, or other uncertainty measures reported for estimated or calibrated parameters.

source_page_refs.quote: For calibration/estimation results, a measure of uncertainty in the estimated parameters

items.item_name: Report confidence in the generality of the optimization result
items.item_question: Does the manuscript report some measure of confidence that the reported optimization result is not a fragile or narrowly local finding?
items.item_question_background: The paper notes that global optima often cannot be guaranteed in simulation optimization. It therefore asks for indicators of how general or reliable the reported optimum is, such as consistency across restarts or robustness under different random streams.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any measure of confidence in the generality of the optimization result, such as unique local optima discovered, restart outcomes, or evidence that additional search is unlikely to find substantially better solutions.

source_page_refs.quote: A measure of confidence in the generality of optimization results
A global optimum cannot usually be guaranteed in simulation optimization

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A system dynamics paper reports model equations, parameter values, units, simulation platform, scenario settings, stochastic iterations, and optimization procedures and needs a reproducibility-focused reporting framework.
A computational social science manuscript presents simulation experiments with sensitivity analysis, uncertainty summaries, and platform-specific details needed for independent reproduction.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely theoretical social-science manuscript with no computational model, simulation runs, or optimization experiments is not an RGSR manuscript.
An empirical observational study that only uses standard statistical regression without a simulation model or scenario-based computational experiments should not map to RGSR.
Matching.Explain: Use RGSR for social-science manuscripts in which simulation models generate reported results and readers need enough detail to reproduce the model, rerun experiments, and assess optimization procedures.
